dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for chest pain at 12340 [STREET_ADDRESS], [CITY]. Respond now.
Good morning! I'm Jordan, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Chest pain started around 0800 this morning, and it's moving to my left arm.
Mr. Hargrove, open your eyes for me please. Can you tell me your name?
Jerry… Jerry Hargrove.
Where are we now, Mr. Hargrove?
I think it's [STATE]. I'm not quite sure the town.
What is the date today?
October 12th, I think.
And what made you call 911?
Well, chest pain brought things up again. It migrated down my left arm just now.
Mr. Hargrove, we're going to do a quick check of your throat and windpipe. Any difficulty breathing?
No-no difficulty breathing, just pain.
Did you have anything in your mouth recently like gum or candy?
Nothing unusual, just some floss and brushing this morning.
Can you take a deep breath and let me listen to your lungs? Put your hand here.
Yes, deep breath it is.
Good chest expansion, lungs are clear on both sides. Any wheezing or coughing?
No wheezing.
Mr. Hargrove, we are going to take your blood pressure. Just a snug cuff on your arm, okay?
Blood pressure is 188 over 104.
Respirations are at 18 breaths per minute.
Pulse is 90 and strong.
SpO2 is at 99% with no oxygen on board.
Skin is warm with a normal tone. Let me check for signs of jugular venous distention.
No jugular venous distention observed. We noticed a scar on your chest. Any history related to that?
He had a shunt put in a year ago.
Mr. Hargrove, can you tell me any medications you currently take on a daily basis?
Furosemide, aspirin, protonix, and coreg.
What other medications or treatments have you used today or recently?
I took two nitro pills before you got here. These alleviated a little pain.
When did the chest pain start for you today?
Around 0800. Took some meds, but it's still bothering me.
What kind of activities or other things make the pain worse or better?
Just sitting here it feels okay. Through my arm, it restricts movement.
How would you describe the pain in your chest? Is it sharp, dull, crushing?
It's like a frame collapsed across my left side and weight pressed deep in.
Does the pain spread or radiate anywhere else, such as your neck or jaw?
Definitely into my left arm. No noticeable movement elsewhere.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst pain you've ever felt, how intense is the pain now?
Definitely close to a 10.
How long has this pain lasted for you?
Since maybe an hour or two after beginning at 0800 this morning.
Before this occurred, have you had similar issues in the past?
Uh, I had a heart attack in 2012 and a minor stroke last year.
Have you had any other heart or lung issues, or surgeries, that's in your memory?
I must have had some hypertension all this time.
What did you do before this all began this morning?
Slept through the night. Coffee was routine, then this alarm with athletics kicked in.
Going now to look at your neck and head area. No resistance-this should be painless.
No Battle's sign, normal pupillary size and shape. Neck seems anatomical without signs of trauma.
Letting me know if you feel any pain when I press on your chest.
Going to listen to your heart now. Any pain here at all?
Heart sounds are clear. No extra sounds heard on either the bell or diaphragm.
Chest is symmetrical with no signs of lobar taps or pain.
On to your stomach. No touch will be harsh - just gentle palpitation, okay?
Yes, here you go.
No hard masses or rigidity observed in your belly. No signs of unprompted pain.
Let's look at your limbs now. Did you sprain or twist anything lately for pain?
Nope, nothing happened to my limbs, convenient to use as is.
Your hands are warm, and skin is intact. All extremities are moving freely.
I'll have a quick peek at your back. Just bare skin without pulling clothes entirely.
All right.
No bruising, lumps, or strange pigmentation. Spine area looks unaltered.
Skin is cooler on the left ears, otherwise skin is normal.
Mr. Hargrove, per your symptoms, we will be following a chest pain protocol. Can you inform us of a hospital you prefer?
Mr. Hargrove, per instruction, do you want us to take you to either [CITY] or UVA Hospital?
Take me to [CITY] as quickly as you can.
Mr. Hargrove, we will now check your blood sugar. You may feel a slight feeling on the fingertip.
The first glucose reading was not available due to protocol; second reading is 161 mg/dl.
We are now going to monitor your heart with an EKG. Three pads going to be placed, painless.
The EKG is showing a sinus rhythm with occasional premature ventricular contractions.
Also, signs of mild left ventricular hypertrophy and possible lateral and inferior ischemia are noted.
We have also obtained a 12-lead EKG as part of our assessment.
We've placed you on 4-lead ECG monitoring to track your heart rhythm carefully.
GCS is 15 for Mr. Hargrove - he's alert, oriented.
No jugular venous distention present. Head and neck examination finds no swelling or other signs of injury.
Can we administer a 324mg of aspirin now?
Yes, anything you say is fine.
Alright, we are preparing a lineup of IV fluids to stay ready if you need a fast response treatment en route.
20G IV has been started on the left arm with NS KVO. Initial 50cc has been infused now.
Let's check back now. Blood pressure is 184 over 88.
Pulse is now 85, and stable.
Resp is 18, still even. Oxygen at 100% with ambient air.
EKG recheck doesn't show deterioration. Sinus rhythm continues, with fewer PVCs.
Mr. Hargrove, how is your chest pain feeling now?
Slight relief, but still hurting a fair amount in my left arm.
Pupil and breathing checks remain good. Significance of IV was net-administered as precaution.
Due to the driveway condition, four of us will assist in rolling you in safely.
We will stabilize you with a semi-Fowler's position on the stretcher. Did you bring any personal items for comfort?
Just my glasses. Keep them in the pocket there.
Rolling carefully now. Mr. Hargrove, look up and away from the gravel for safety.
Now securing you with safety straps on stretcher. Feeling stable for travel now.
Calling hospital now, [COMPANY_NAME]. Here is the initial report.
Male patient with chest pain, moving to the left arm. Has CAD, old MI, hypertension, and a history of stroke.
Currently on IV fluids, aspirin administered.
Hospital says they're ready for arrival. Any more comments before we begin transport?
I appreciate it all. Let's move what needs to be moved.
Mr. Hargrove, let's check your responsiveness again. Are you oriented to person, place, and time?
I know who I am, I know we are in [STATE], and I think it's October 12th.
Glucose was unavailable at first due to protocol, but we obtained a reading of 161 mg/dl later.
Mr. Hargrove, the report says you had nausea and diarrhea - were you throwing up or going to the bathroom frequently this morning?
Yes, I felt nauseated since Wednesday and threw up this morning with no blood in the vomit. I also had some diarrhea.
You're also taking carvedilol. Are you currently on any other medications I might have missed?
That's all.
